patient.name: Jane Doe
patient.medications: Lisinopril 10 mg daily, Atorvastatin 20 mg daily

data.result: No evidence of acute intracranial hemorrhage. Age-related cerebral atrophy noted. A small area of hypodensity is seen in the left MCA territory, suggestive of early ischemic changes.

data.findings: Right-sided weakness (4/5), expressive aphasia, facial droop on the right side. NIHSS score: 12.
Regular heart rate and rhythm, no murmurs auscultated.
Clear and equal breath sounds bilaterally.

data.interventions: Assess neurological status q15 minutes during alteplase infusion, then q30 minutes for 6 hours, then q1 hour for 18 hours. Monitor for changes in level of consciousness, motor strength, and speech.
Maintain BP <185/110 mmHg. Administer antihypertensive medications as prescribed. Monitor BP q15 minutes during and after alteplase administration.
Monitor for signs of bleeding (e.g., gums, nose, urine, stool). Avoid invasive procedures if possible. Apply pressure to puncture sites for at least 10 minutes.

clinicalFeed:
S: This is the nurse for Jane Doe, a 70-year-old female who presented at 14:00 with acute right-sided weakness and aphasia. 
B: Last known well was at 13:00. She has a history of hypertension and hyperlipidemia. 
A: Current BP is 190/100 mmHg. A non-contrast head CT shows no hemorrhage but a small area of hypodensity in the left MCA territory. The assessment is an acute ischemic stroke within the thrombolytic window. 
R: Recommend initiating measures to lower BP to <185/110 mmHg to proceed with IV alteplase administration per protocol.
stem: The nurse is managing care for the client who presented with acute neurological deficits. The client has been diagnosed with an acute ischemic stroke and is a candidate for thrombolytic therapy. Complete the bowtie by selecting the appropriate actions to take and parameters to monitor.

hoverRationales.cond1: Pathophysiology: Sudden focal neurological deficits combined with a CT scan negative for hemorrhage strongly indicate an ischemic stroke, where a clot obstructs cerebral blood flow, leading to neuronal injury.
hoverRationales.cond2: Clinical Flaw: A brain tumor typically presents with a gradual onset of symptoms, headaches, or seizures, not the sudden, focal deficits seen in this client.
hoverRationales.cond3: Clinical Flaw: A Transient Ischemic Attack (TIA) involves temporary symptoms that resolve completely, usually within an hour. This client's symptoms are persistent, indicating a completed stroke.
hoverRationales.cond4: Clinical Flaw: A hemorrhagic stroke would show evidence of bleeding on the non-contrast CT scan. This client's CT scan was negative for hemorrhage, ruling out this condition.
hoverRationales.action1: Clinical Flaw: A lumbar puncture is not indicated for diagnosing an acute ischemic stroke and is contraindicated if intracranial pressure is elevated, as it increases the risk of cerebral herniation.
hoverRationales.action2: Pathophysiology: Blood pressure must be lowered to and maintained below 185/110 mmHg before and during alteplase administration to decrease the risk of hemorrhagic conversion, a major complication of thrombolytic therapy.
hoverRationales.action3: Clinical Flaw: Anticoagulants like heparin are contraindicated in the acute phase of ischemic stroke, especially when thrombolytics are used, as they significantly increase the risk of intracranial hemorrhage.
hoverRationales.action4: Pathophysiology: Alteplase is a thrombolytic that dissolves the fibrin clot causing the ischemia. Administering it within the therapeutic window restores blood flow to the penumbra, minimizing neuronal death.
hoverRationales.param1: Pathophysiology: Continuous BP monitoring is essential to ensure the pressure remains within the target range, which is critical for balancing the need for cerebral perfusion against the risk of hemorrhagic transformation.
hoverRationales.param2: Clinical Flaw: While monitoring for systemic bleeding is important, the most critical parameters to detect the life-threatening complication of hemorrhagic conversion are frequent neurological assessments and blood pressure monitoring.
hoverRationales.param3: Clinical Flaw: This is only one component of the comprehensive neurological assessment. Monitoring the full NIHSS and overall neurological status provides a more complete picture of the client's condition.
hoverRationales.param4: Pathophysiology: Frequent neurological assessments using the NIHSS are critical to monitor for improvement or deterioration, which reflects changes in cerebral perfusion and helps detect complications like hemorrhage early.

pearls.title: Time is Brain
pearls.description: In acute ischemic stroke, every minute counts. Rapid assessment and intervention are crucial to minimize brain damage and improve patient outcomes.
pearls.strategy: Prioritize rapid neurological assessment, CT imaging, and initiation of thrombolytic therapy (if indicated) within the appropriate time window.

pearls.title: Blood Pressure Control
pearls.description: Maintaining blood pressure within the target range (<185/110 mmHg) is essential before, during, and after alteplase administration to reduce the risk of hemorrhagic conversion.
pearls.strategy: Administer antihypertensive medications as prescribed and monitor blood pressure closely, adjusting medications as needed to maintain the target range.

traps.title: Ignoring Subtle Neurological Changes
traps.description: Subtle changes in neurological status can indicate improvement, deterioration, or complications such as hemorrhagic conversion. Overlooking these changes can delay critical interventions.
traps.strategy: Perform frequent and thorough neurological assessments using the NIHSS and document all findings. Report any changes to the healthcare provider immediately.

traps.title: Administering Anticoagulants
traps.description: Administering anticoagulants like heparin in the acute phase of ischemic stroke, especially when thrombolytics are used, significantly increases the risk of intracranial hemorrhage.
traps.strategy: Ensure that anticoagulants are not administered unless specifically indicated and prescribed by the healthcare provider. Double-check medication orders and patient history to avoid errors.

mnemonics.description: Used to recognize stroke symptoms: Face drooping, Arm weakness, Speech difficulty, Time to call 911.
mnemonics.strategy: Educate patients and families about the FAST mnemonic to promote early recognition of stroke symptoms and prompt medical attention.

mnemonics.description: Blood Pressure Target: <185/110 mmHg before, during, and after alteplase administration.
mnemonics.strategy: Use the BP-TARGET mnemonic to remember the target blood pressure range and ensure that blood pressure is effectively managed during stroke care.

physicalExam.cardiovascular: Regular heart rate and rhythm, no murmurs auscultated.
physicalExam.respiratory: Clear and equal breath sounds bilaterally.

radiology.ctHead: No evidence of acute intracranial hemorrhage. Age-related cerebral atrophy noted. A small area of hypodensity is seen in the left MCA territory, suggestive of early ischemic changes.

medications.labetalol: 10-20 mg IV PRN for BP >185/110 mmHg

Maintain BP <185/110 mmHg. Administer antihypertensive medications as prescribed. Monitor BP q15 minutes during and after alteplase administration.
Monitor for signs of bleeding (e.g., gums, nose, urine, stool). Avoid invasive procedures if possible. Apply pressure to puncture sites for at least 10 minutes.
